zones.name: Hench Agent Core
zones.description: Agent orchestration layer: task briefing, multi-turn LLM loop, completion validation, review flow, and run persistence.
insights: High cohesion (0.97) — files are tightly interconnected
Clean control-plane zone that makes decisions and orchestrates workflows without directly executing filesystem or shell operations.
High cohesion (0.97) reflects tight internal coupling among loop, brief, prompt, and completion modules that collectively drive the agentic cycle.
Entry points (tools/rex.ts, schema/index.ts, schema/v1.ts) expose the rex integration tool and type contracts consumed by the tooling zone.

Hench has a hard npm dependency on rex ("rex": "*" in package.json) and imports 5 distinct rex internal modules (store/types, store/index, core/next-task, core/tree, core/timestamps, core/parent-completion, schema/v1) across 6 source files — this is a deep library-level coupling, not a subprocess or data-level relationship.

The hench→rex dependency serves three purposes: task selection (brief.ts uses findNextTask/findItem), status mutation (tools/rex.ts writes through PRDStore), and store initialization (run.ts calls resolveStore). All three would need to change if rex's internal APIs shift.

zones.name: Hench Agent Tooling
zones.description: Sandboxed tool implementations (file I/O, shell, git) and security guard subsystem that validates every agent action.
insights: High cohesion (0.84) — files are tightly interconnected
Guard subsystem (~165 lines across 3 files) enforces path traversal prevention, command allowlisting, and shell operator rejection for every tool invocation.
Each tool (files.ts, git.ts, shell.ts) is a stateless async function with no inter-tool dependencies, enabling independent testing and extension.
Lower cohesion (0.84) compared to the core zone reflects the guard subsystem's role as a cross-cutting concern consumed by all tool implementations.

The single back-reference from tooling→core (rex.ts importing completion.ts for validateCompletion) creates the bidirectional coupling — this exists because the rex tool needs to validate task completion before marking status, mixing tool execution concerns with agent-level judgment.

guard/index.ts imports GuardConfig from schema/index.ts (in hench-agent-core zone), meaning the guard subsystem depends on the core zone for its configuration type — this is a necessary structural dependency since schema is the canonical type source.

zones.name: Rex Analysis Pipeline
zones.description: Project scanning, deduplication, LLM-enriched proposal generation, and conflict reconciliation pipeline for PRD item discovery.
insights: Clean linear pipeline: scanners → dedupe → propose → reason (LLM) → reconcile → diff, with each stage consuming the previous stage's output.
Highest coupling in the codebase (0.23) is structurally necessary: the analysis pipeline must read the existing PRD tree and schema to avoid proposing duplicates.
The analyze CLI command lives in this zone alongside its implementation, giving the feature good vertical cohesion.

rex-analysis has zero external package consumers — only rex CLI commands (analyze.ts and smart-add.ts) import it, making it a purely internal feature zone with a well-defined single entry point (analyze/index.ts).

scanSourceVision in scanners.ts defines its own local TypeScript interfaces (SVFinding, SVZone, SVZonesData, SVInventoryData, SVImportsData) that mirror sourcevision's output format rather than importing shared types — this is a deliberate data-contract decoupling that trades type safety for package independence.

zones.name: Rex PRD Core
zones.description: PRD data model, tree operations, storage adapters (file and Notion), state machine, sync engine, schema validation, and all non-analyze CLI commands.
insights: High cohesion (0.97) — files are tightly interconnected
Foundational layer with 7 entry points serving distinct clients (CLI commands, analyze pipeline, external tools), which is appropriate for an 78-file infrastructure zone.
Store abstraction cleanly separates file-based and Notion persistence behind a PRDStore interface, enabling adapter swapping without touching consumers.
Generator-based tree traversal in core/tree.ts provides memory-efficient, composable operations across the PRD hierarchy.

Rex exposes its internals (store/types.ts, core/tree.ts, core/next-task.ts, core/timestamps.ts, core/parent-completion.ts, schema/v1.ts) as de facto public API consumed by hench, but has no explicit public API surface — hench imports via rex/dist/* paths directly into implementation modules.

The store layer (FileStore, NotionStore, SyncEngine, AdapterRegistry) is the most externally consumed subsystem — both rex CLI commands and hench's run.ts depend on resolveStore(), making it the primary integration point.

zones.name: Root Orchestration
zones.description: Top-level CLI dispatcher, CI pipeline runner, and unified configuration manager for the n-dx toolkit.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Three standalone scripts with no shared internal module; each independently spawns sub-tool processes.
config.js is the largest file (~460 lines) and handles cross-package config reads and writes, making it a potential maintenance hotspot if new packages are added.
No entry points detected because these files are consumed as a top-level executable (cli.js shebang) and direct imports, not as library exports.

config.js breaks the subprocess-only pattern by directly reading/writing .rex/config.json, .hench/config.json, and .sourcevision/manifest.json via fs/promises, creating a second coupling mechanism (filesystem convention) alongside the subprocess spawning used by cli.js and ci.js.

ci.js introduces a third coupling variant: subprocess spawning with stdout capture and JSON parsing, meaning the three files use three distinct integration styles (inherit stdio, capture+parse JSON, direct file I/O).

zones.name: Sourcevision Analysis Engine
zones.description: Core static analysis pipeline: file inventory, import graph extraction, Louvain zone detection, React component cataloging, AI enrichment, and output generation (CONTEXT.md, llms.txt).
insights: High cohesion (0.97) — files are tightly interconnected
Schema layer (data-files.ts, validate.ts) serves as the clean contract boundary to the viewer — all cross-zone imports route through it

Enrichment subsystem (enrich.ts, enrich-config.ts, enrich-parsing.ts, claude-cli.ts) is ~800 lines with retry/batching/timeout logic and could benefit from extraction into a dedicated src/enrichment/ subdirectory
Entry points (data-files.ts, validate.ts, next-steps.ts) are precisely the files the viewer needs — filenames, validation, and recommendations — confirming a well-factored producer/consumer split
Strictly unidirectional dependency: analysis engine never imports from viewer zone — all cross-zone flow routes through schema/v1.ts, data-files.ts, and validate.ts as intermediaries

zones.name: Sourcevision Test Fixture
zones.description: Minimal TypeScript project (7 files) used as a deterministic integration test baseline for validating the analysis pipeline produces correct inventory, import graphs, and zone output.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling confirm complete isolation — this fixture is an inert input artifact, not a runtime dependency
The fixture covers layered architecture (models/services/utils) and shared-hub imports but omits circular dependencies, dynamic imports, and React components
Integration tests (pipeline.test.ts, llms-txt.test.ts, context.test.ts) rely on this single fixture — a second fixture with adversarial patterns would strengthen regression coverage
Fixture dependency graph is strictly acyclic with a single shared-hub pattern (user-service.ts imports from 3 modules) — no circular dependencies, dynamic imports, barrel files, or React/JSX present

zones.name: Sourcevision Viewer
zones.description: Preact-based interactive web UI with force-directed graph visualization, zone/file browsers, architecture views, and dual-mode data loading (dev server or drag-and-drop static files).
insights: High cohesion (0.96) — files are tightly interconnected
The graph subsystem (physics.ts + renderer.ts, ~600 lines of imperative SVG/math) bypasses the Preact VDOM for performance — this is the highest-risk viewer code and has minimal test coverage
schema/v1.ts is the sole entry point from the analysis zone, confirming the viewer is a pure consumer of JSON artifacts with no back-channel into the analysis pipeline
Modular CSS with design tokens (tokens.css) and dedicated accessibility styles (a11y.css) shows mature frontend engineering for a dev tool

Viewer has 5 component-level tests (BarChart, FlowDiagram, TreeView, DetailPanel, CollapsibleSection) but the graph subsystem (physics.ts + renderer.ts) has zero test coverage despite being the highest-risk imperative code
Bidirectional coupling: "rex-analysis" ↔ "rex-prd-core" (14+4 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "hench-agent-core" ↔ "hench-agent-tooling" (4+4 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "sourcevision-analysis-engine" ↔ "sourcevision-viewer" (3+4 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Size imbalance: largest zone has 78 files vs smallest with 3 — uneven decomposition

The codebase follows a consistent pattern of splitting packages into a large core/infrastructure zone and a smaller specialized feature zone, seen in hench (core+tooling), rex (analysis+core), and sourcevision (3 zones).
Cross-zone coupling is low across the board (max 14 imports) and predominantly unidirectional, indicating healthy dependency flow from feature zones toward foundational zones.
All split-package zone pairs (hench/hench-2, rex/rex-2) show the expected pattern: the larger zone has higher cohesion and lower coupling, serving as the stable base.

All three sourcevision zones communicate exclusively through the schema layer (v1.ts types, validate.ts, data-files.ts constants), forming a clean producer-consumer architecture: analysis engine writes JSON, viewer reads JSON, test fixture provides input

Cross-zone import counts are symmetric and low (3-4 each direction between sourcevision and sourcevision-2), matching the rex and hench zone pairs — this monorepo consistently splits packages into a core processing zone and a presentation/integration zone

The sourcevision package's 3-zone split (engine, viewer, fixture) mirrors the hench package's 2-zone split (core, tooling) and rex's 2-zone split (analysis, PRD core), suggesting Louvain reliably finds the natural seams in each package

The dependency graph is strictly unidirectional at the package level: hench→rex (library imports), rex→sourcevision (filesystem data reads), root→all (subprocess spawning) — no reverse dependencies exist.

Sourcevision is the only fully leaf-node package: zero inbound code imports, zero outbound code imports — it communicates exclusively through .sourcevision/*.json data files read by rex's scanners and the recommend command.

The monorepo uses three distinct integration patterns at decreasing coupling strength: library imports (hench→rex), filesystem data contracts (rex→sourcevision via JSON files), and subprocess spawning (root-orchestration→all) — each chosen appropriately for the relationship's stability requirements.

Hench's lack of any sourcevision dependency means it cannot directly leverage codebase analysis for task execution — all sourcevision context must be pre-baked into rex PRD items by the plan phase before hench can act on it.

Hench has 9 runtime imports from rex (findNextTask, findItem, resolveStore, computeTimestampUpdates, findAutoCompletions, etc.) making it the only cross-package runtime dependency in the monorepo — sourcevision and rex are fully standalone

All three packages implement an identical CLIError class (Error subclass with optional suggestion string) in cli/errors.ts independently — this is intentional duplication preserving per-package deployability, not an extraction candidate

All three packages use Zod for schema validation with an identical { ok: true; data: T } | { ok: false; errors: ZodError } result type pattern but share no validation code — the pattern convergence is a de facto convention without a shared library

Rex and hench both implement identical project-config override logic (loadProjectOverrides + mergeWithOverrides from .n-dx.json) in store/project-config.ts — this duplicated config-merge code is a realistic extraction candidate into a shared workspace utility

The monorepo dependency graph is a strict DAG: sourcevision has zero cross-package imports, rex has zero cross-package imports, and hench depends on rex at runtime — no cycles exist at the package level

findings.text: Bidirectional coupling: "hench-agent-core" ↔ "hench-agent-tooling" (4+4 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "rex-analysis" ↔ "rex-prd-core" (14+4 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "sourcevision-analysis-engine" ↔ "sourcevision-viewer" (3+4 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Size imbalance: largest zone has 78 files vs smallest with 3 — uneven decomposition
High cohesion (0.97) — files are tightly interconnected
High cohesion (0.84) — files are tightly interconnected
High cohesion (0.97) — files are tightly interconnected
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
High cohesion (0.97) — files are tightly interconnected
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
High cohesion (0.96) — files are tightly interconnected

All six package-internal zone pairs (rex↔rex-2, hench↔hench-2, sourcevision↔sourcevision-2) show 3-4 cross-zone imports each, indicating consistent boundary discipline across the monorepo. No zone pair exceeds 14 imports (rex→rex-2), and that outlier reflects rex-2's larger size (78 files).
Bidirectional coupling exists in hench (4+4) and rex (14+4) zone pairs but is type-contract based in both cases, not circular business logic; no runtime risk.

Rex-analysis has the highest coupling ratio (0.23) in the codebase; while structurally justified, growth of the analysis pipeline should be monitored to prevent it from becoming tightly bound to core internals.

Test fixtures appearing as first-class architectural zones (sourcevision-3) is a Louvain artifact — the algorithm correctly identifies them as disconnected communities but they are not architectural zones. Consider filtering tests/fixtures/** paths before zone detection to produce cleaner output for consumers.
The root orchestration zone has zero coupling to any package zone, maintaining clean separation between the CLI entry point and package internals.

Three zones across the monorepo have coupling ≤ 0.04 to their sibling zones (sourcevision-viewer, hench-tooling at 0.84 cohesion, rex-analysis at 0.77 cohesion), with the two lower-cohesion zones in rex and hench suggesting those boundaries may be less natural than sourcevision's clean engine/viewer split.
Bidirectional coupling with hench-agent-tooling (4 imports each way) is type-contract based, not circular business logic, so it poses no runtime risk.
Schema types (including GuardConfig) are defined in this zone but consumed primarily by hench-agent-tooling; extracting a shared types layer would eliminate the bidirectional coupling between zones.
The agent loop files (loop.ts, cli-loop.ts, brief.ts) are well-sized at 250-560 lines each, avoiding monolithic orchestrators.
Security-by-default architecture: guard validates every tool call, with no bypass path. This is well-suited for an autonomous agent operating on user codebases.
The 0.16 coupling ratio is the second-highest in the codebase, driven by guard config types imported from the core zone's schema; a shared interface file would reduce this.
Tool dispatch in agent/tools.ts uses a switch statement across ~7 tools; this scales adequately now but a registry pattern would be warranted if the tool count exceeds 12.
All 14 cross-zone imports into rex-prd-core are unidirectional (analysis reads core, core does not import analysis), which is the correct dependency direction for a pluggable feature.
Cohesion at 0.77 is the lowest of all zones, indicating the analysis pipeline has some internal fragmentation; the CLI command file bridges two concerns (CLI orchestration and pipeline invocation).

reason.ts handles LLM integration with retry logic, JSON repair, and chunking in a single module; if it grows further, extracting JSON repair and chunking as standalone utilities would improve testability.
High cohesion (0.97) and minimal outbound coupling (0.03) confirm this zone is a stable foundation that other zones depend on without being destabilized by them.
State machine logic is well-distributed: transitions.ts enforces status rules, parent-completion.ts cascades, dag.ts prevents cycles — no single file owns too much responsibility.

sync-engine.ts (552 lines) and notion-map.ts (726 lines) are the two largest files and handle inherently complex bidirectional sync; documenting the sync algorithm phases would aid future maintainers.
Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling confirm these files form a self-contained orchestration layer with no inbound dependencies from packages.
ci.js and config.js are imported only by cli.js; consider co-locating them in a dedicated orchestration directory if more top-level commands are added.

Cohesion of 0.97 with only 3 cross-zone imports confirms tight internal wiring. All external surface goes through the schema layer, which is the ideal pattern for a pipeline that produces JSON artifacts.

Large analyzer files (zones.ts ~400 lines, imports.ts ~350 lines) combine detection logic with metrics computation and insight generation. Splitting by concern (extraction vs. metrics vs. insights) would reduce cognitive load without changing the pipeline flow.

The enrichment subsystem (enrich.ts + 3 helpers) mixes orchestration, prompt construction, CLI invocation, and response parsing in adjacent files without a clear subdirectory boundary. As this is the most failure-prone subsystem (network, auth, parse errors), isolating it would improve testability and error tracing.

The fixture is intentionally simple (7 files, no cycles, no React) to serve as a baseline. This means the analysis pipeline's handling of circular dependencies, dynamic imports, and component detection is only tested against real-world codebases, not controlled fixtures.

The fixture's layered structure (index → services → models + utils) with a shared hub (format.ts imported from two locations) exercises the most common real-world import pattern, making it a solid baseline despite its simplicity.

Zero coupling means Louvain correctly identified this as a completely disconnected community. Consider excluding tests/fixtures/** from zone detection to avoid surfacing test data as an architectural zone in production output.

Coupling of 0.04 (4 imports into the analysis engine) routes exclusively through schema files (data-files.ts, validate.ts). The viewer never imports analyzers or CLI code, preserving its ability to run as a standalone static app.

The graph physics engine and SVG renderer (~600 lines) are imperative code that mutates DOM directly, sidestepping Preact's reconciliation. Unit tests cover only flow diagrams, not the core force simulation or zoom/pan/highlight logic. This is the highest-risk area for regressions.

View files (graph.ts, zones.ts, files.ts) at 200-300 lines each mix rendering and state management. Extracting data-preparation logic into utility functions would improve testability without requiring an architectural change.

All cross-package data contracts (sourcevision JSON files, rex PRDStore interface, rex schema types) flow in a single direction matching the execution pipeline: sourcevision produces → rex consumes and produces → hench consumes — no package produces data consumed by an upstream package.
All three packages converge on the same schema/validate.ts + schema/v1.ts + cli/errors.ts structural pattern, establishing a de facto package template without a shared generator or scaffolding tool

Duplicated project-config merge logic in rex/src/store/project-config.ts and hench/src/store/project-config.ts performs identical .n-dx.json loading and deep-merge — extractable to a shared workspace package

The three integration mechanisms (subprocess, filesystem JSON, library import) form a coupling gradient that mirrors package stability: most-stable sourcevision uses loosest coupling (data files), moderately-stable rex uses medium coupling (subprocess + data), and the tightest coupling (library imports) exists only between the two most co-evolved packages (hench→rex).

The PRDStore interface from rex/dist/store/types.js is threaded through brief.ts, loop.ts, cli-loop.ts, and tools/rex.ts as a constructor parameter, acting as the primary dependency-inversion boundary between hench and rex.

All tool implementations (files.ts, git.ts, shell.ts) are stateless functions with identical signatures (params + guard → result), but rex.ts breaks this pattern by also importing agent-layer logic (validateCompletion, formatValidationResult).
scanners.ts duplicates sourcevision type definitions locally instead of sharing them, creating a shadow type contract that could silently drift if sourcevision's JSON schema evolves.

The smart-add.ts command imports from the analyze zone, creating a second consumer of the analysis pipeline beyond the primary analyze.ts command — this prevents the analyze zone from being treated as a single-consumer internal module.

Three files use three different integration mechanisms with sub-packages: cli.js spawns with inherited stdio, ci.js spawns and parses JSON stdout, config.js does direct filesystem I/O — no shared abstraction for package communication.
next-steps.ts lives inside analyzers/ but produces output consumed exclusively by the viewer — it is functionally a boundary module misplaced in the producer zone

Fixture omits edge cases that the analysis engine handles: circular dependencies, dynamic import(), re-exports, barrel index files, and React component detection — a second adversarial fixture would improve regression coverage
Graph subsystem (physics.ts, renderer.ts) bypasses Preact VDOM with imperative SVG/math and has zero test coverage — this is the most fragile viewer code path

Hench→Rex runtime coupling (9 function imports across 6 files) is the sole cross-package dependency — if rex core APIs change, hench breaks with no compile-time safety beyond TypeScript path resolution against dist/

Rex has no explicit public API module (index.ts barrel) despite being consumed as a library by hench — hench reaches into rex/dist/store/types, rex/dist/core/*, and rex/dist/schema/* directly, creating a fragile integration surface.

Rex's shadow type definitions for sourcevision data (SVFinding, SVZone, etc. in scanners.ts) and the absence of a shared schema package mean the sourcevision↔rex data contract has no compile-time enforcement and could drift silently.

Hench imports rex core utilities (findNextTask, findItem, walkTree, computeTimestampUpdates, findAutoCompletions) that are implementation details rather than public API — these are internal algorithms that could change without notice.

tools/rex.ts importing agent/completion.ts is the sole cause of bidirectional coupling between hench zones — extracting validateCompletion into a shared module or the schema layer would make the dependency unidirectional.

The analysis pipeline imports only 3 symbols from rex-prd-core (PRDItem type, Priority type, walkTree function), while the reverse direction (CLI commands importing analyze) pulls in ~15 symbols — the dependency is heavily asymmetric despite being bidirectional.
Hench imports 5+ internal rex modules via rex/dist/* paths with no public API barrel file — any internal refactoring in rex could break hench without warning since there is no stable API boundary.
config.js must understand the JSON schema of every package's config file, creating implicit coupling to all three packages' internal config formats without compile-time safety.
